name: spec-to-green
purpose: Turn a fuzzy feature request into a verifiable spec WITH a deterministic check, then drive code to green against that check — so 'done' is proven, not claimed. It operationalizes Day 4/5: evaluation-gated, the check is written before the code.
inputs: A natural-language task or feature request; the target repo (its test/check command auto-detected); optional acceptance hints. If the repo has no check command, the tool co-defines one.
outputs: 1) a spec file (objective + acceptance criteria as machine-checkable assertions); 2) a check command or script that encodes those criteria; 3) a diff that makes the check pass; 4) a short trajectory log of what was tried (Day 4: the trajectory is the truth).
when_to_use: A task is fuzzy or multi-step, correctness matters, and you want proof-of-done rather than a confident 'looks good'. Ideal as the Specification->Validate stages of the hub loop.
when_not_to_use: A one-line, obviously-correct change (writing a check is ceremony); pure exploration/spikes where the goal is learning, not shipping; tasks whose success is inherently subjective (route those to a human gate, not this tool).
workflow: Restate the intent in one sentence + name what 'done' looks like (Intent).
Detect the repo's verification source: objective registry > project check/test command > co-define 3-7 criteria with the human (Plan).
Write the spec: each acceptance criterion as a deterministic assertion; add the check FIRST and watch it fail red (Specification).
Make the smallest change that turns one criterion green; re-run the check (Execute).
Read each failure, fix the named file, re-run; stop after the same failure 3x and escalate with what was tried (Validate, bounded loop).
When green, score the trajectory against the Four Pillars and surface any human-judgment gate; never self-certify subjective quality (Evaluate).
Record one decision + one lesson to org memory so the next run starts smarter (Learn/Decide).
validation: The repo's check command exits 0 AND every acceptance assertion is exercised by it. Deterministic: a human can re-run `bash scripts/check.sh` (or the detected command) and reproduce green. No green is faked; an honest red beats a bad fix.
failure_handling: Missing info -> ask <=2 clarifying questions at genuine forks, else state the assumption explicitly and proceed. Tool/command fails -> report the exact error + last file touched, do not paper over it. Same failure 3x -> halt, escalate with the trajectory. Low confidence on a subjective criterion -> hand it to a human gate rather than guess.
